function.name: web_search
function.description: Search the public internet for information. Each result will contain a url, a title, and one excerpt taken directly from the page.

start_date.description: (optional) The start date for the search range in the format YYYY-MM-DD. Must not be equal to end_date.

end_date.description: (optional) The end date for the search range in the format YYYY-MM-DD. If the value is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

number_of_results.description: (optional) The number of search results to return.

function.name: retrieve_information
function.description: Retrieves critical data segments from a previously ingested document via hybrid lexical and semantic calculations. Crucial Requirement: The search prompt argument MUST contain the token placeholder containing your storage key inside double curly brackets, exactly format formatted as: {{key_name}} alongside your query description text.

prompt.description: Your target question containing the explicit target reference mapping (e.g., 'What were the total research costs in {{my_document_key}}?').

preserve_document_order.description: Set to True if processing complex structural data dependencies across sequential disclosures.

function.name: parse_html_page
function.description: Parses an HTML document or SEC filing. It automatically extracts key metadata, converts asset tables to clean markdown, slices sections into optimal word chunks, and runs concurrent contextual LLM enrichment before updating the disk cache.

url.description: The target URL of the HTML page/filing to process.

key.description: The unique cache storage key for saving and loading the index.

function.name: edgar_search
function.description: Search the EDGAR Database through the SEC API. You should provide a search query. You can also optionally provide a start date, an end date, a page number, top N results, a list of form types, and/or a list of CIKs. The results are returned as a list of dictionaries, each containing the metadata for a filing. It does not contain the full text of the filing.

search_query.description: The case-insensitive search-term or phrase to search the contents of filings and their attachments. This can be a single word, phrase, or combination of words and phrases. Supported search features include wildcards (*), Boolean operators (OR, NOT), and exact phrase matching by enclosing phrases in quotation marks ("exact phrase"). By default, all terms are joined by an implicit AND operator.

form_types.description: (optional) Limits search to specific EDGAR form types (e.g., ['8-K', '10-Q']) list of strings. Default: all form types

ciks.description: (optional) Filters results to filings from specified CIKs, type list of strings. Leading zeros are optional but may be included. Example: [ "0001811414", "1318605" ]. Default: all CIKs

start_date.description: (optional) Start date for the search range in yyyy-mm-dd format. If the value is a date that is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

end_date.description: (optional) End date for the search range, in the same format as startDate. If the value is a date that is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

page.description: (optional) Used for pagination. Each page contains up to 100 matching filings. Increase the page number to retrieve the next set of 100 filings. Example: 3 retrieves the third page. Default: 1

top_n_results.description: (optional) Return only the first N results out of 100 from the page. If not provided, all 100 results will be returned. E.g. if page is 2, and top_n_results is 10, you will receive results 100 to 110.

function.name: calculator
function.description: Evaluate a mathematical expression and return the result. Use this tool for all arithmetic calculations instead of computing by hand. Supports: +, -, *, /, ** (exponentiation), % (modulo), and parentheses for grouping. Available functions: abs(), min(), max(), sqrt(), log(), log10(). Examples: '(5000 - 3200) * 0.21', '(2865507 / 1905871) ** 0.5 - 1', '14060 / 2148'.

function.name: price_history
function.description: Fetch historical daily price data for a specific asset class. Returns a CSV table with one row per day. Use asset_class='equity' or 'etf' for US-listed stocks/ETFs (e.g. AAPL, SPY), 'crypto' for pairs like btcusd (lowercase, no dash), or 'fx' for pairs like audusd. Non-US equities and most indices/futures are not covered by the pricing provider. Each row includes raw OHLC (open/high/low/close), split- and dividend-adjusted OHLC (adjOpen/adjHigh/adjLow/adjClose), raw and adjusted volume, divCash (dividend amount on the date), and splitFactor.

ticker.description: Ticker symbol. High-level symbology rules: (1) Equity/ETF/mutual fund: use the US exchange-listed symbol (AAPL, SPY, VFIAX). Symbols are case-insensitive and contain only letters and dashes. (2) Share classes use a dash, not a dot: 'BRK-A' / 'BRK-B' (not 'BRK.A'), 'BF-B'. (3) Securities are identified by the ticker as listed on the primary US exchange; non-US listings, OTC pink sheets, indices, and futures are generally not covered. (4) Tickers can be reused after delisting; data is point-in-time for the entity that held the ticker on each date. (5) Crypto: concatenated lowercase pair with no separator, e.g. 'btcusd', 'ethusd'. (6) FX: concatenated lowercase pair with no separator, e.g. 'audusd', 'eurusd'.

asset_class.description: Asset class used to route to the proper pricing endpoint.

function.name: submit_final_result
function.description: Submits the final answer to the user. You should include your final answer, as well as any necessary reasoning, justification, calculations, and explanation. Finally, you should provide any sources used to answer the question. You MUST use this tool to submit your final result. The user will not see your response if you do not use this tool to submit. You will not be able to continue working after this tool is called; the conversation will be ended.

final_result.description: The final result to submit to the user

steps.message:
You are an IPO and financial diligence agent.

You are given a question and must answer it using the tools provided.

You cannot interact with the user or ask clarifying questions.

Answer solely based on available information and tools.

Assume the current date is May 31, 2026.

---

TOOLS AND DATA SOURCES

You have access to a document storage system.

You may store parsed HTML retrieved from the web and later retrieve
information using LLM-based retrieval prompts.

Use this system to avoid context-window limitations.

When working with long filings, prefer section-specific retrieval prompts
rather than broad document-level retrieval.

Examples:

- retrieve the Segment Adjusted EBITDA reconciliation table from MD&A
- retrieve the glossary definition of Starshield
- retrieve the controlled-company governance disclosure

For peer-comparison questions:

- retrieve peer SEC filings using edgar_search
- do not rely on memory for peer financial figures

For historical public-equity price data not available in SEC filings:

- use price_history as primary source
- use raw unadjusted prices unless adjusted prices are requested
- fall back to web_search only if price_history fails

Stock indices are not covered by price_history.

Use authoritative sources such as FRED or web_search for index levels.

---

SOURCE HIERARCHY

Prioritize sources in this order:

1. S-1 / S-1A filings
2. Incorporated SEC filings
3. SEC exhibits and merger documents
4. SEC periodic filings
5. Company earnings materials
6. External sources

If figures differ across sources:

prefer the most authoritative SEC source containing the directly reported
figure and explain material discrepancies when relevant.

SEC filings are the primary and most authoritative source of financial and
disclosure information.

---

ANSWER STANDARDS

You must call submit_final_result with the completed answer.

Your submission will not be processed unless submit_final_result is called.

Include:

- complete reasoning
- calculations
- supporting analysis

You will be evaluated on:

- final-answer accuracy
- analytical correctness

Do not round intermediate calculations.

Round only final reported values.

When possible, report calculated figures to at least two decimal places.

Report financial figures using the same scale and units used in the filing
unless otherwise specified.

Share prices should be reported with two decimal places.

---

DISCLOSURE DISCIPLINE

Before reaching a conclusion:

determine whether the filing provides sufficient information to answer:

- directly
- partially
- only through inference

Distinguish carefully among:

- facts explicitly disclosed
- reasonable filing-supported inferences
- information not disclosed

Do not speculate beyond what the filing supports.

If material information is absent:

explicitly state that the filing does not provide sufficient information to
conclude.

Absence of disclosure may itself be analytically material.

---

FORWARD-LOOKING STATEMENTS

Treat separately:

- historical facts
- roadmap targets
- commercialization expectations
- management intentions
- forward-looking statements

Do not present aspirations or plans as historical outcomes.

---

GOVERNANCE AND LEGAL QUESTIONS

For governance, legal, or disclosure-language questions:

use the filing's precise terminology whenever possible.

Distinguish among:

- requirements
- exemptions
- company elections
- forward-looking language

---

ACCOUNTING POLICY QUESTIONS

For accounting-policy questions:

1. Identify the relevant disclosed accounting policy or treatment
2. Determine whether the filing explicitly resolves the issue
3. If unresolved, state that the filing does not specify treatment

---

NON-GAAP QUESTIONS

For non-GAAP measures:

identify:

- the starting GAAP metric
- each disclosed adjustment
- whether economically recurring costs are excluded

Do not accept non-GAAP measures without analyzing their construction.

---

AUDIENCE TAGS

Questions may include audience tags such as:

[PUBLIC | BANK]
[VC | CREDIT]
[LEGAL | ACCOUNTING]

Use these tags to calibrate terminology and analytical emphasis while
remaining grounded in disclosed facts.

---

SOURCES

At the end of your answer provide:

{
  "sources": [
    {
      "url": "...",
      "name": "..."
    }
  ]
}


---


FILING CONTEXT

The primary filing for this benchmark is the SpaceX S-1 filed May 20, 2026:

https://www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/1181412/000162828026036936/spaceexplorationtechnologi.htm

Important:

The consolidated financial statements have been retrospectively recast under
common-control accounting to include:

- xAI (merged February 2, 2026)
- X Holdings (merged into xAI March 28, 2025)

for all historical periods presented.

No period reflects standalone SpaceX operations exclusively.

When interpreting historical segment figures, state whether they reflect the
recast consolidated entity.

SpaceX (SPCX) has no public trading history as of May 30, 2026.

Do not attempt price_history retrieval for SpaceX or SPCX.

For peer valuation or peer financial benchmarking, retrieve peer-company SEC
filings using edgar_search rather than relying on memory.

Question:
Using FY2025 Connectivity segment figures, calculate YoY growth in revenue, operating income, and Segment Adjusted EBITDA. What do the differential growth rates imply about operating leverage within Connectivity?

steps.reasoning_content:
The user is asking me to calculate YoY growth in revenue, operating income, and Segment Adjusted EBITDA for the Connectivity segment of SpaceX for FY2025. I need to find FY2025 and FY2024 figures for these metrics from the SpaceX S-1 filing.

Let me first parse the S-1 filing and then retrieve the relevant segment information.

- Operating Income: $4,423 million
- Segment Adjusted EBITDA: $7,168 million

FY2024:
- Revenue: $7,599 million
- Operating Income: $2,006 million
- Segment Adjusted EBITDA: $3,849 million

Now let me calculate YoY growth rates:

Revenue growth: (11,387 - 7,599) / 7,599 = 3,788 / 7,599
Operating Income growth: (4,423 - 2,006) / 2,006 = 2,417 / 2,006
Segment Adjusted EBITDA growth: (7,168 - 3,849) / 3,849 = 3,319 / 3,849

Let me calculate these.

Now let me also calculate the margins to understand the operating leverage story better.

FY2025 margins:
- Operating margin: 4,423 / 11,387
- Segment Adjusted EBITDA margin: 7,168 / 11,387

FY2024 margins:
- Operating margin: 2,006 / 7,599
- Segment Adjusted EBITDA margin: 3,849 / 7,599

Let me also calculate the difference between Segment Adjusted EBITDA and Operating Income to understand D&A and other adjustments.

Now let me also retrieve more details about the Connectivity segment to understand what's driving the growth and operating leverage. Let me also check if there's more detail about the segment's cost structure, D&A, etc.

arguments.prompt: Retrieve details about the Connectivity segment business description, Starlink subscriber growth, and any discussion of operating leverage, cost structure, or margin expansion from {{spacex_s1}}
Retrieve the Segment Adjusted EBITDA reconciliation table showing the adjustments from operating income to Segment Adjusted EBITDA for the Connectivity segment from {{spacex_s1}}

results.content:
Based on the provided financial and operational highlights, here are the details regarding the Connectivity segment's business description, Starlink subscriber growth, and discussions on operating leverage, cost structure, and margin expansion:

### **1. Connectivity Segment Business Description**
The Connectivity segment is primarily driven by **Starlink**, which provides global, high-speed, low-latency internet access via a Low-Earth Orbit (LEO) constellation. As of March 31, 2026, the segment operated over 9,600 broadband and mobile satellites. The business is divided into several key offerings:
*   **Consumer Broadband:** Operates the world’s largest space-based internet service, offering fiber-like download speeds (median 225 Mbps during peak hours) and low latency (~25 milliseconds). While initially focused on rural, remote, and underserved communities, the value proposition is expanding into suburban and urban environments as network capacity grows.
*   **Enterprise Solutions:** Provides critical primary or backup connectivity to industries such as aviation, maritime, construction, agriculture, retail, telecom, and hospitality. Major customers include United Airlines, Carnival, Maersk, and John Deere. 
*   **Mobile Connectivity:** Offers satellite-to-mobile texting and over-the-top voice services to supplement terrestrial networks and eliminate "dead zones" across approximately 30 countries.
*   **Revenue Model:** Generates revenue through monthly subscription fees (varying by geography and speed), upfront terminal/kit sales, and enterprise contracts based on subscriptions, data consumption, or capacity.

### **2. Starlink Subscriber Growth**
Starlink has experienced explosive user adoption, driven by global brand awareness, expanded retail distribution, and new product offerings:
*   **Subscriber Base:** As of March 31, 2026, Starlink reached approximately **10.3 million subscribers** across 164 countries/territories. This represents a massive **~105% year-over-year growth** from 5.0 million subscribers in Q1 2025 (and up from 2.3 million at the end of 2023).
*   **Mobile Users:** The satellite-to-mobile service has scaled to approximately **7.4 million monthly unique devices**.
*   **ARPU Dynamics:** Rapid subscriber growth has been accompanied by a decline in Average Revenue Per User (ARPU). ARPU fell to $66 in Q1 2026 (down 22.9% YoY) and averaged $81 for FY2025 (down 11.2% YoY). This decline is a strategic result of international expansion into emerging markets and the introduction of lower-priced, localized service plans to drive mass adoption.

### **3. Operating Leverage, Cost Structure, and Margin Expansion**
The financial highlights demonstrate significant operating leverage and a highly scalable cost structure, though the company continues to invest heavily in next-generation capacity.

**Margin Expansion & Operating Leverage:**
*   **Outsized Profit Growth:** In FY2025, the Connectivity segment's revenue grew by **49.8%** (to $11.38 billion), but its Income from Operations surged by **120.4%** (to $4.42 billion) and Adjusted EBITDA grew by **86.2%** (to $7.16 billion). This divergence between revenue growth and profit growth highlights strong operating leverage.
*   **Drivers of Leverage:** The company attributes this sequential EBITDA margin expansion to the "scale and efficiency of our LEO satellite constellations and our highly verticalized supply chain." 

**Cost Structure Advantages:**
*   **Minimal Marginal Costs:** Unlike terrestrial networks that require massive incremental capital expenditures to run cables to low-density areas, Starlink’s space-based architecture enables "economically scalable service delivery across these regions with minimal marginal cost."
*   **Launch & Hardware Depreciation:** A major component of the segment's cost of revenue is the depreciation of capitalized launch and satellite costs. However, the company is actively mitigating this through the increased reusability of its Falcon launch vehicles, which has successfully lowered depreciation expenses in the Space segment.
*   **Operating Expenses:** Cost increases are currently being driven by ground operating costs, customer support, payment processing, freight, warranty costs, and targeted marketing/international expansion costs required to onboard new users.

**Future Cost & Capacity Catalysts (Starship & V3 Satellites):**
*   The company is heavily funding R&D for the **Starship** launch vehicle and next-generation **V3 satellites** (offering 1 Tbps of downlink capacity per satellite). 
*   A single Starship launch will be capable of deploying up to 60 V3 satellites, representing a **twenty-fold increase** in downlink capacity deployed relative to a standard Falcon 9 launch. This is expected to drastically improve network efficiency, lower the cost-per-bit, and further expand margins as enterprise and urban adoption scales.

| Segment Adjusted EBITDA ................................ | $653 |  | $7,168 |  | $(1,237) |  | $6,584 |

**Margins:**

**Reconciliation (D&A and SBC):**

|---|---|---|---|

Key findings:

2. Operating Income grew 120.5% YoY (2.4x revenue growth rate)
3. Segment Adjusted EBITDA grew 86.2% YoY (1.7x revenue growth rate)

The differential growth rates imply strong operating leverage. The gap between operating income growth (120.5%) and Adj. EBITDA growth (86.2%) is explained by D&A growing faster than revenue (57.6% vs 49.8%), reflecting the capital-intensive satellite deployment cycle.

arguments.final_result:
## Connectivity Segment — FY2025 YoY Growth Analysis

### Summary Financial Table (Recast Consolidated Basis)

| Metric | FY2024 | FY2025 | YoY Growth |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Revenue** | $7,599M | $11,387M | **+49.8%** |
| **Operating Income** | $2,006M | $4,423M | **+120.5%** |
| **Segment Adjusted EBITDA** | $3,849M | $7,168M | **+86.2%** |

### Margin Expansion

| Margin Metric | FY2024 | FY2025 | Change |
|---|---|---|---|
| Operating Margin | 26.4% | 38.8% | **+1,240 bps** |
| Segment Adj. EBITDA Margin | 50.7% | 62.9% | **+1,230 bps** |

### Segment Adjusted EBITDA Reconciliation Bridge

| Component | FY2024 | FY2025 | YoY Growth |
|---|---|---|---|
| Operating Income | $2,006M | $4,423M | +120.5% |
| + Depreciation & Amortization | $1,508M | $2,376M | +57.6% |
| + Share-Based Compensation | $296M | $369M | +24.7% |
| + Impairment | $39M | $0M | — |
| **= Segment Adjusted EBITDA** | **$3,849M** | **$7,168M** | **+86.2%** |

---

### Operating Leverage Analysis

The differential growth rates reveal **powerful and multi-layered operating leverage** within the Connectivity segment:

**1. Operating Income grew 2.4× faster than revenue (120.5% vs. 49.8%).**
This is the clearest signal of operating leverage. As Starlink's subscriber base scaled to ~10.3 million (approximately +105% YoY), the segment's largely fixed-cost satellite network infrastructure allowed incremental revenue to flow through to operating profit at a dramatically higher rate. The ~1,240 bps operating margin expansion (from 26.4% to 38.8%) confirms that the marginal cost of serving additional subscribers is well below the segment's average cost, consistent with the filing's description of "minimal marginal cost" for the LEO satellite architecture.

**2. Segment Adjusted EBITDA grew 1.7× faster than revenue (86.2% vs. 49.8%), but materially slower than Operating Income.**
The gap between Operating Income growth (+120.5%) and Adj. EBITDA growth (+86.2%) is explained by **D&A growing at +57.6%**, outpacing revenue growth. This reflects the capital-intensive satellite deployment cycle — as SpaceX continues to launch new satellites (over 9,600 in orbit as of Q1 2026), the associated capitalized costs are depreciated, creating a growing D&A charge that partially offsets operating leverage at the EBITDA level. D&A expanded from $1,508M to $2,376M (+$868M), absorbing roughly 23% of the incremental revenue.

**3. SBC grew only +24.7%, well below revenue growth.**
Share-based compensation grew from $296M to $369M — a modest increase that further contributed to margin expansion, indicating that headcount-related equity costs are not scaling proportionally with revenue.

**4. ARPU headwinds make the leverage story even more impressive.**
The filing discloses that FY2025 average ARPU declined ~11.2% YoY (to $81/month) as Starlink expanded into emerging markets with localized, lower-priced plans. Despite this per-unit pricing pressure, the segment still achieved massive margin expansion — indicating that volume-driven scale effects and cost efficiencies far outweighed ARPU dilution.

### Implications

The differential growth rates imply that Connectivity has passed the **inflection point** typical of capital-intensive network businesses:

- **Near-term:** The fixed-cost nature of the satellite constellation means each incremental subscriber contributes high-margin revenue. Operating leverage should persist as the subscriber base grows, though the rate of margin expansion may moderate as the segment scales.
- **D&A as a headwind to EBITDA leverage:** The faster growth of D&A vs. revenue means that Operating Income will continue to exhibit greater leverage than Adj. EBITDA. This gap will narrow only when the satellite deployment cadence stabilizes or when next-generation Starship/V3 satellites (with 20× the downlink capacity per launch) drive down cost-per-bit.
- **Structural advantage:** The combination of a scalable space-based architecture, vertical integration, and declining launch costs (via Falcon reusability and future Starship) positions Connectivity for sustained margin expansion — a rare profile for a business still growing revenue at ~50%.

---

**Sources:**

{
  "sources": [
    {
      "url": "https://www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/1181412/000162828026036936/spaceexplorationtechnologi.htm",
      "name": "SpaceX S-1 Registration Statement (filed May 20, 2026)"
    }
  ]
}

## Connectivity Segment — FY2025 YoY Growth Analysis

### Summary Financial Table (Recast Consolidated Basis)

| Metric | FY2024 | FY2025 | YoY Growth |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Revenue** | $7,599M | $11,387M | **+49.8%** |
| **Operating Income** | $2,006M | $4,423M | **+120.5%** |
| **Segment Adjusted EBITDA** | $3,849M | $7,168M | **+86.2%** |

### Margin Expansion

| Margin Metric | FY2024 | FY2025 | Change |
|---|---|---|---|
| Operating Margin | 26.4% | 38.8% | **+1,240 bps** |
| Segment Adj. EBITDA Margin | 50.7% | 62.9% | **+1,230 bps** |

### Segment Adjusted EBITDA Reconciliation Bridge

| Component | FY2024 | FY2025 | YoY Growth |
|---|---|---|---|
| Operating Income | $2,006M | $4,423M | +120.5% |
| + Depreciation & Amortization | $1,508M | $2,376M | +57.6% |
| + Share-Based Compensation | $296M | $369M | +24.7% |
| + Impairment | $39M | $0M | — |
| **= Segment Adjusted EBITDA** | **$3,849M** | **$7,168M** | **+86.2%** |

---

### Operating Leverage Analysis

The differential growth rates reveal **powerful and multi-layered operating leverage** within the Connectivity segment:

**1. Operating Income grew 2.4× faster than revenue (120.5% vs. 49.8%).**
This is the clearest signal of operating leverage. As Starlink's subscriber base scaled to ~10.3 million (approximately +105% YoY), the segment's largely fixed-cost satellite network infrastructure allowed incremental revenue to flow through to operating profit at a dramatically higher rate. The ~1,240 bps operating margin expansion (from 26.4% to 38.8%) confirms that the marginal cost of serving additional subscribers is well below the segment's average cost, consistent with the filing's description of "minimal marginal cost" for the LEO satellite architecture.

**2. Segment Adjusted EBITDA grew 1.7× faster than revenue (86.2% vs. 49.8%), but materially slower than Operating Income.**
The gap between Operating Income growth (+120.5%) and Adj. EBITDA growth (+86.2%) is explained by **D&A growing at +57.6%**, outpacing revenue growth. This reflects the capital-intensive satellite deployment cycle — as SpaceX continues to launch new satellites (over 9,600 in orbit as of Q1 2026), the associated capitalized costs are depreciated, creating a growing D&A charge that partially offsets operating leverage at the EBITDA level. D&A expanded from $1,508M to $2,376M (+$868M), absorbing roughly 23% of the incremental revenue.

**3. SBC grew only +24.7%, well below revenue growth.**
Share-based compensation grew from $296M to $369M — a modest increase that further contributed to margin expansion, indicating that headcount-related equity costs are not scaling proportionally with revenue.

**4. ARPU headwinds make the leverage story even more impressive.**
The filing discloses that FY2025 average ARPU declined ~11.2% YoY (to $81/month) as Starlink expanded into emerging markets with localized, lower-priced plans. Despite this per-unit pricing pressure, the segment still achieved massive margin expansion — indicating that volume-driven scale effects and cost efficiencies far outweighed ARPU dilution.

### Implications

The differential growth rates imply that Connectivity has passed the **inflection point** typical of capital-intensive network businesses:

- **Near-term:** The fixed-cost nature of the satellite constellation means each incremental subscriber contributes high-margin revenue. Operating leverage should persist as the subscriber base grows, though the rate of margin expansion may moderate as the segment scales.
- **D&A as a headwind to EBITDA leverage:** The faster growth of D&A vs. revenue means that Operating Income will continue to exhibit greater leverage than Adj. EBITDA. This gap will narrow only when the satellite deployment cadence stabilizes or when next-generation Starship/V3 satellites (with 20× the downlink capacity per launch) drive down cost-per-bit.
- **Structural advantage:** The combination of a scalable space-based architecture, vertical integration, and declining launch costs (via Falcon reusability and future Starship) positions Connectivity for sustained margin expansion — a rare profile for a business still growing revenue at ~50%.
